title: Psalms 93
subtitle: The LORD Reigns

sections.header: Verses 1–2 — “The LORD Is Robed in Majesty”

heb.gloss: he reigns / he has become king
heb.paragraph: "The LORD reigns (mālāk̅). He is robed in majesty; the LORD is robed in majesty and armed with strength" (v.1). The first enthronement psalm (93, 95–99). God’s reign is described through clothing metaphors: robed in majesty, armed with strength. The world is established; it cannot be moved (v.1). God’s throne is established from of old; he is from all eternity (v.2).

notes.note: "The LORD reigns. He is robed in majesty." Enthronement declaration. God does not merely exist; he REIGNS. The world is stable because the king is dressed for action.
"Your throne was established long ago; you are from all eternity." The throne predates creation. The king was reigning before anything existed to reign over.

refs.note: The great multitude shouting 'our Lord God Almighty reigns' echoes the psalm's enthronement declaration.
The Lord God Almighty beginning to reign fulfills the psalm's kingship theology.
David's song declaring 'the LORD reigns' uses identical enthronement language.

hist.context: Psalm 93 begins the 'enthronement psalms' (93-99) celebrating YHWH's kingship. The declaration 'the LORD reigns' (YHWH malak) may have functioned in New Year festivals reenacting divine kingship. The imagery of God robed in majesty, establishing the world, and subduing chaotic waters draws on ancient Near Eastern royal and creation mythology.

sections.header: Verses 3–5 — “Mightier Than the Breakers of the Sea”

heb.paragraph: "Mightier (ʾaddîr) than the thunder of the great waters, mightier than the breakers of the sea — the LORD on high is mighty" (v.4). The seas raise their voice (v.3) — chaos threatens. But God is mightier than the waves. The psalm closes: "Your statutes, LORD, stand firm; holiness adorns your house for endless days" (v.5). Two permanences: God’s word stands; God’s house is holy.

notes.note: "The seas have lifted up their voice; the seas have lifted up their pounding waves. Mightier than the thunder of the great waters — the LORD on high is mighty." Chaos vs sovereignty. The waves roar; God is louder.
"Your statutes stand firm; holiness adorns your house for endless days." Permanence of word + permanence of holiness. Both endure.

refs.note: The LORD enthroned over the flood parallels this psalm's mastery over mighty waters.
Jesus rebuking wind and waves demonstrates the divine authority over chaos the psalm celebrates.
No more sea in the new creation completes the conquest of chaotic waters.

hist.context: The 'seas' and 'mighty waters' lifting their voice (vv.3-4) represent chaos threatening cosmic order. YHWH's might 'on high' surpasses these forces. The closing declaration that God's statutes 'stand firm' and holiness adorns his house (v.5) connects cosmic sovereignty to moral law and temple worship.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God is a key figure in The LORD Reigns.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVMightier than the thunder of the great waters, mightier than the breakers of the sea— the Lord on high is mighty.ESVMightier than the thunders of many waters, mightier than the waves of the sea, the LORD on high is mighty!NIVYour statutes, Lord, stand firm; holiness adorns your house for endless days.ESVYour decrees are very trustworthy; holiness befits your house, O LORD, forevermore.

rec.text: ""The LORD reigns. He is robed in majesty." Enthronement declaration. God does not merely exist; he REIGNS. The world is stable because the king is dressed for action."

rows.label: Mightier Than Waves — 93:3–5
rows.text: "Mightier than the breakers of the sea." Statutes stand firm. Holiness for endless days.

hebtext.gloss: he reigns / he has become king
hebtext.note: "The LORD reigns (mālāk̅). He is robed in majesty; the LORD is robed in majesty and armed with strength" (v.1). The first enthronement psalm (93, 95–99). God’s reign is described through clothing metaphors: robed in majesty, armed with strength. The world is established; it cannot be moved (v.1). God’s throne is established from of old; he is from all eternity (v.2).

"Mightier (ʾaddîr) than the thunder of the great waters, mightier than the breakers of the sea — the LORD on high is mighty" (v.4). The seas raise their voice (v.3) — chaos threatens. But God is mightier than the waves. The psalm closes: "Your statutes, LORD, stand firm; holiness adorns your house for endless days" (v.5). Two permanences: God’s word stands; God’s house is holy.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10: THE LORD REIGNS. Holiness: adorns his house for endless days. The first enthronement psalm.

prayer_prompt: The LORD reigns; the world is firmly established. Consider asking God about the stability you're seeking — is it found in circumstances or in His unchanging reign?
